old_claim.note: Filters & Membranes (Early) is an earlier historical predecessor or foundation, not a one-to-one engineering dependency.

new_claim.note: Hemodialysis machines require a semipermeable membrane; Kolff's rotating-drum artificial kidney used cellophane tubing immersed in dialyzing fluid.

source_shape.source_locator: Kolff and Scribner Nature Medicine/Lasker commentary, artificial-kidney birth section.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The source describes urea removal through cellophane tubing, then a rotating-drum artificial kidney with cellophane tubing immersed in dialyzing fluid and used on a patient in 1943.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: This supports a required membrane edge because diffusion across the semipermeable tubing is the separation mechanism that makes hemodialysis work, not merely historical background.

ontology_before: Dialysis machines treated early membranes as a loose historical predecessor.
ontology_after: Dialysis machines now treat semipermeable filtration membrane capability as the hard separation-method foundation.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Changed: the membrane edge is required rather than a generic historical predecessor.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to a non-membrane renal-support method rather than dialysis machines.
A source showed hemodialysis machines can perform blood purification without a semipermeable separation interface.
The graph removed clinical treatment context while making membranes required.
short_reason: Hemodialysis is built around membrane separation, so the membrane edge is required.
